context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m kind of lost on the Spider Diagram assignment for my city case study. I don’t really get what counts as the “5 Pathways for Urban Transformation” or how detailed each part is supposed to be.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re not stuck on the city yet—you’re stuck on the framework. Fix that first.

Think of the 5 Pathways as 5 big buckets the course uses to organize urban climate action. Your job is not to write everything about the city, just to sort what the city is doing into those buckets.

Start here: what are the 5 Pathways listed in your course materials or lecture notes? Paste them here exactly as the course names them, and then we’ll figure out what kind of city actions belong under each one.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will stay in the tutor role and not complete the assignment for the student. Since the student is confused about the framework, I should reduce ambiguity without filling in their case study content. I will use guided discovery by first narrowing the task into manageable parts, asking the student to identify what they already know, and prompting them to connect their city evidence to the course framework. I will keep the response brief, focused on the assignment, and ask a Socratic question that moves directly toward completing the Spider Diagram.

exchanges.student: I think that’s part of why I’m confused — I don’t know if I wrote them down right, and the course terms all blur together for me. I remember they were kind of like governance/policy, buildings or infrastructure, transport maybe, and social stuff? I’m not sure what counts as a “pathway” versus just an example.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—your confusion is about levels, not effort.

A pathway is a broad category of transformation. An example is a specific action, policy, project, or condition that fits inside that category.

Test yourself: is “public transit expansion” a big bucket, or is it one thing a city might do? Is “governance” a bucket, or one action?

Answer those two, and then tell me your case study city so we can sort its actions into the right level.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for conceptual clarification, so I should help distinguish category from example without supplying the assignment content or pretending certainty about course-specific labels. I will use guided discovery by having the student infer the difference between a pathway and an action/example, and I will anchor them back to course materials. I will keep it bite-sized and move toward a concrete next step for filling the spider diagram.

exchanges.student: I think public transit expansion is one thing a city might do, and governance is the bigger bucket. But then I start second-guessing everything, because “infrastructure” also sounds like a huge bucket and also like an action area. My city is Medellín, but I still feel like I don’t know how broad each section is supposed to be.
exchanges.tutor:
That first distinction was correct. Stop second-guessing it.

Use this rule: each pathway = broad theme; inside it, you list a few concrete Medellín actions, plans, actors, or problems that fit. Not everything. Just enough to map the city.

Try one test: pick one Medellín climate-related thing you already know. What is it, and is it a broad theme or a specific example?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a correct distinction but is still uncertain about scope. I should validate the productive part, then guide them to use a simple scope rule for the spider diagram: each pathway is broad, and each entry should be a few concrete city-relevant items, not exhaustive detail. I must avoid supplying the full framework or content for Medellín. A focused Socratic prompt will help them generate one example and test whether it fits.

exchanges.student: I know Medellín has the cable cars and public transit integration, so I guess that’s a specific example, not a broad theme. But then I don’t know if I’m supposed to explain why it matters for climate, or list like every related transport project too.
exchanges.tutor:
Right: that’s a specific example. Don’t turn the diagram into a full essay.

Ask yourself: what is the minimum needed to show why that example belongs in the pathway? Usually: the example + a short note on its climate relevance. Not every transport project.

So for the cable cars/transit integration, what’s the climate connection you think the course wants you to notice?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking about expected level of detail. I should clarify scope without doing the work: likely brief mapping, not exhaustive cataloging. I will use Socratic questions to help them infer what minimal useful detail is needed for a spider diagram entry and keep them focused on assignment purpose.

exchanges.student: Maybe that it reduces car use or makes lower-carbon transportation easier? I’m just worried because then I can kind of say that about a lot of things, and I don’t know how specific or academic the wording has to be.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s the right kind of connection. The goal is not fancy wording; it’s accurate sorting.

For each item, aim for: what it is + how it connects to climate action. Clear beats “academic-sounding.”

Don’t ask, “Does this sound scholarly?” Ask, “Would a reader see why this belongs here?”

Now do one in your own words: for Medellín transit, what is the action, and what is the climate link?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is anxious about specificity and wording. I should reduce anxiety by emphasizing function over polished language, while refusing to write for them. I will guide them to focus on evidence + connection rather than sounding academic. A Socratic prompt will have them draft the core idea in their own words.

exchanges.student: I’d probably write something like: Medellín’s integrated transit system, including cable cars, supports lower-carbon mobility by improving access to public transportation and reducing reliance on private cars. But I still feel like I’m guessing the format, because I can’t picture how that turns into a whole spider diagram without me just repeating the same kind of sentence five times.
exchanges.tutor:
That idea is workable. Your problem now is structure, not content.

A spider diagram is repetitive in format, but each section should point to a different kind of city change. Don’t vary the sentence style—vary the type of thing you include.

Ask: for each pathway, am I naming a different domain of transformation?

Can you list 3 different domains you think Medellín shows, even roughly, without worrying yet about perfect course labels?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a workable example but is now concerned about overall structure and repetition. I should affirm the conceptual progress without polishing their wording, then redirect to the assignment's organizing principle: one distinct type of evidence per pathway. I will use guided discovery by asking them to differentiate pathways by kind of action/actor/system instead of sentence template. I will stay brief and avoid writing the diagram for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe transportation, governance/planning, and green space or environmental management? I’m still fuzzy because I could also put some of the same Medellín stuff under equity or infrastructure, so it feels like everything overlaps and I don’t know how not to do it wrong.
exchanges.tutor:
Overlap is normal. Don’t chase perfect separation.

Use this rule: put each example in the pathway where its main mechanism of change sits. You can mention overlap briefly, but choose one primary home.

So for Medellín’s integrated transit, what is the primary mechanism: mobility/infrastructure, governance, or equity? Why that one first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is dealing with overlap, which is common in categorization tasks. I should normalize overlap, give a rule for primary placement, and use a Socratic prompt to have them justify one categorization. I must not provide the actual pathway labels or fill in Medellín content for them. Keep it concise and focused on the assignment.

exchanges.student: I’d put it under mobility/infrastructure first, because the main thing is changing how people move around the city, even if it also affects equity and planning. But I’m still not sure how many examples I’m supposed to have under each pathway, and I feel like if I pick too few it’ll look empty.
exchanges.tutor:
That categorization makes sense. Good.

Don’t invent a quota if the course doesn’t give one. A section is sufficient when it shows the pathway is visible in Medellín through a few concrete items—or shows a gap if evidence is weak.

Ask yourself: for each pathway, do I have enough to show what Medellín is doing, who is involved, and why it matters for climate?

How many examples do you currently have for your strongest pathway?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a sound categorization and now worries about adequacy. Since I should not invent assignment requirements, I need to avoid claiming an exact number unless given by the course. I will steer them toward sufficiency criteria: enough to show evidence of action or gaps in each pathway. I will ask a targeted question that helps them self-assess completeness.

exchanges.student: Honestly, I’m not sure because I haven’t really separated them out yet. I just have a messy pile of notes and links, and that’s why I keep wanting a clearer picture of what a “finished” spider diagram is supposed to look like overall.
exchanges.tutor:
Then stop thinking about the polished final product. You’re still at the sorting stage.

A finished spider diagram is just: 5 pathway sections, each with a small set of Medellín notes placed in the most fitting bucket. That’s it.

Do this next: make 5 rough buckets and sort your notes/links into them fast, even if some items are uncertain.

What are 5 notes from your pile that you could sort first?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stuck at organization. I should move them from abstract worry to a concrete sorting step, since the assignment can be completed via a table and the student likely needs a simple workflow. I will not produce the content, but I can describe the finished product at a high level and ask them to sort notes into buckets. This keeps the guidance minimal and action-oriented.

exchanges.student: I have notes on integrated transit/cable cars, air pollution, the Green Corridors project, city climate plans, and informal settlements on hillsides with landslide risk. I can sort some of those, but then I get stuck because air pollution feels like an outcome, landslide risk feels like a vulnerability, and I’m not sure if the diagram wants problems, actions, or both.
exchanges.tutor:
Good—now you’re actually working with the assignment.

Use this distinction: some notes are conditions/problems, others are responses/actions. A strong pathway section can include both, as long as the problem helps explain the action.

Test your 5 items: which are mainly vulnerabilities/conditions, and which are mainly city responses?

Sort them into those 2 groups first. Then we’ll place them into pathways.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student now has concrete notes and is confused about what kinds of items belong in the spider diagram. I should clarify the organizing logic without supplying the final categorization. I will use guided discovery to separate problems/vulnerabilities from actions/strategies and show that both can be useful if tied to the pathway. I will keep it brief and ask the student to classify their five notes, which moves directly toward building the diagram.
